name: Richard Boykin
bio: Richard R. Boykin is a Democratic candidate for Illinois' 7th Congressional District in the March 17, 2026 primary. Boykin grew up in Englewood on Chicago's South Side, where he was an All-City and All-State football player at Chicago Vocational High School. He earned a B.A. from Central State University (Ohio) on a football scholarship and a J.D. from the University of Dayton School of Law. He served as chief of staff to U.S. Rep. Danny K. Davis from 1997 to 2006 before winning election to the Cook County Board of Commissioners, District 1 in 2014 (term began per county records). He served on the Board until December 3, 2018, when he lost his reelection primary in a razor-thin margin to future Chicago Mayor Brandon Johnson (50.4%–49.6% — a margin of 437 votes). He ran for Cook County Board President in 2018 against incumbent Toni Preckwinkle, losing in the primary, and ran for Cook County Clerk of the Circuit Court in 2020, finishing third (24.9%) behind Iris Martinez (33.7%) and Michael Cabonargi (27%). He ran again for Cook County Board President in 2022, losing to Preckwinkle 75.8%–24.2%. Now running for Congress in the seat his former boss Danny Davis held for nearly 30 years; Davis himself has endorsed La Shawn Ford. Boykin is a lawyer and presents himself as a practical, experienced political operator with establishment roots and cross-district relationships.
prior_office: Cook County Board of Commissioners, District 1 (2014–2018)

jacket.note: FEC data from committee C00911388 (RICHARD BOYKIN FOR CONGRESS). 84 total receipts. Boykin himself contributed $40,500 in multiple tranches (largest single: $25,000 Jul 2025). Committee heavily concentrated in Oak Park donors. Willie Wilson ($3,500) is a notable name donor. Most donors are local Oak Park/West suburban community members. Total $383,262 raised, $60,641 cash on hand as of Feb 25, 2026.
jacket.donors_note: Itemized donor data pending ETL import from FEC filing H6IL07370. Total confirmed via Ballotpedia/FEC aggregate.

red_flags.label: Lost four consecutive electoral contests since 2018
red_flags.detail: Boykin lost in the 2018 primary for Cook County Commissioner District 1 (to Brandon Johnson, 437-vote margin), the 2018 primary for Cook County Board President (to Preckwinkle), the 2020 primary for Cook County Clerk of the Circuit Court (finished 3rd), and the 2022 primary for Cook County Board President (75.8%–24.2% loss to Preckwinkle). This pattern is a documented electoral record, not an allegation.

key_votes.bill: Cook County Sweetened Beverage Tax ("Soda Tax")
key_votes.summary: Boykin was the first Democrat on the Cook County Board to oppose the sweetened beverage tax introduced by Board President Preckwinkle in 2016. He then led the bipartisan Board majority that voted to repeal it in 2017, over Preckwinkle's strong objection. Widely cited as his signature moment in office.

Boykin initiated a county-wide advisory referendum on expanding mental health treatment funding, placed on the November 2014 ballot. The referendum passed with over 1.4 million votes — a signal of public support for mental health investment he used to advance a policy agenda.

career_history.role: Chief of Staff to U.S. Rep. Danny K. Davis (IL-07)
career_history.org: U.S. House of Representatives, Office of Rep. Danny K. Davis
career_history.highlight: Managed operations for the congressman whose seat Boykin now seeks. Developed relationships across the district and in D.C.
Elected in 2014. Signature act: led repeal of county soda tax in 2017. Lost primary reelection by 437 votes to Brandon Johnson in 2018.
Lost Democratic primary to incumbent Toni Preckwinkle.

career_history.role: Candidate, Cook County Clerk of the Circuit Court
career_history.highlight: Finished 3rd (24.9%) in the Democratic primary, behind Iris Martinez (winner) and Michael Cabonargi.
Boykin has worked as a lawyer throughout his career, including between elected offices.

policy_platform.position: End food deserts, lower drug and health care costs, expand affordable housing, and crack down on corruption so tax dollars flow to district residents, not political insiders.

Launch training and trade programs, prioritize communities with highest unemployment for federal funds, bring manufacturing back to the West Side through tax credits and public funds, support union jobs.
Renew the 1994 Federal Assault Weapons Ban, increase violence prevention program funding, expand mental health services, build community policing partnerships.

social_pulse.summary: Modest but consistent media presence in Chicago political coverage. Known commodity in Cook County Democratic circles. National profile essentially zero. Local coverage focuses on his 'People's Playbook' contrast with establishment.

trust_indicators.label: Long public record — county commissioner, multiple candidate runs — confirmed
FEC filings current and confirmed for 2026 cycle
No ethics investigations or criminal record on public record
Fourth electoral loss since 2018 — pattern of unsuccessful bids
